1.synthesis: Pursue love, and earnestly desire the spiritual gifts, especially that they may prophesy (1 Corinthians 14:1). The one who speaks in a tongue speaks not to men but to God—no one understands, as he utters mysteries in the Spirit (1 Corinthians 14:2). But the one who prophesies builds up the church, encourages, and comforts (1 Corinthians 14:3). The one who speaks in a tongue builds up himself, but the one who prophesies builds up the church (1 Corinthians 14:4). Paul would like them all to speak in tongues but even more to prophesy—for the one who prophesies is greater than the one who speaks in tongues unless he also interprets, so that the church may be built up (1 Corinthians 14:5). If he comes speaking in tongues, how will he benefit them unless he brings some revelation, knowledge, prophecy, or teaching? (1 Corinthians 14:6.) Even lifeless instruments—flute, harp, trumpet—must give distinct notes, otherwise no one knows what is played or what battle is coming (1 Corinthians 14:7–8). If they speak in a tongue, the speech will be speaking into the air—how many kinds of languages are there in the world, yet none is without meaning (1 Corinthians 14:9–10). The one who speaks in a tongue will be a foreigner to the speaker and the speaker a foreigner to him (1 Corinthians 14:11). So with themselves: since they are eager for manifestations of the Spirit, strive to excel in building up the church (1 Corinthians 14:12).
1.historical_context: The Corinthian enthusiasm for tongues reflects a wider Hellenistic religious fascination with ecstatic speech as the sign of divine possession (cf. the Pythia at Delphi, the ecstatic prophets of Dionysus). Paul does not deny the value of tongues—he speaks in tongues more than all of them (v.18)—but subordinates them to intelligibility for the community's sake. The musical instrument analogies (flute, harp, trumpet) appeal to common experience: music without distinct notes is noise; speech without intelligibility is similarly useless. The foreigner language comparison (v.11) demonstrates that the value of language is entirely relational—unknown speech creates social isolation rather than connection.
1.literary_structure: The chapter is structured around a single criterion: what builds up the church? Prophecy is superior to uninterpreted tongues by that criterion (vv.1–5); intelligibility is essential by the same criterion (vv.6–12); the solution for tongues is interpretation (vv.13–19); the outsider's response is the practical test (vv.20–25); ordered worship is the practical conclusion (vv.26–33). This first section establishes the criterion and applies it to the tongues-versus-prophecy question.
1.theological_application: The upbuilding criterion—strive to excel in building up the church (v.12)—is the practical application of love (13:5, love does not insist on its own way) to the gift-exercise question. Tongues as private devotional language between the individual and God is not condemned; its uninterpreted use in corporate worship is problematic because it serves the individual rather than the community. The church's worship must be evaluated by its effect on all who participate: does this build up? Does this encourage? Does this bring comprehension to those who are present? The same criterion applies to preaching, music, liturgical form, and every element of corporate worship. The preference for the spectacular over the intelligible—which characterized the Corinthians—characterizes many communities in every generation, and Paul's corrective remains essential: the gathering of the community is primarily for communal upbuilding, and the gifts should be exercised in service of that purpose.
1.christology: The risen Christ who pours out the Spirit also gives the diversity of gifts and determines their right use. Prophecy is 'greater' (v.5) in the corporate context not because it is more impressive but because it does what the Spirit's gift-distribution is designed to do: build up the body of Christ. The 'speaking to God' of tongues (v.2) is a genuine spiritual reality—Paul affirms it and practices it (v.18)—but in the corporate assembly the Spirit's purpose is communication of the risen Christ's truth to those who are gathered. The intelligibility principle reflects the nature of the incarnation itself: the eternal Word became flesh and spoke in human language so that his Father's character and purposes could be understood by human beings. The Spirit's gifts in corporate worship continue this communicative pattern—they are for understanding, not mystification.

13.synthesis: The one who speaks in a tongue should pray that he may interpret (1 Corinthians 14:13). If Paul prays in a tongue, his spirit prays but his mind is unfruitful. He will pray with his spirit but also with his mind; he will sing with his spirit but also with his mind (1 Corinthians 14:14–15). Otherwise, if someone is in the position of an outsider, how can he say the Amen to the thanksgiving when he does not know what is being said? (1 Corinthians 14:16–17.) In church Paul would rather speak five words with his mind to instruct others than ten thousand words in a tongue (1 Corinthians 14:19). Brothers, do not be children in thinking—be infants in evil but mature in thinking (1 Corinthians 14:20). In the Law it is written: 'By people of strange tongues and by the lips of foreigners will I speak to this people, and even then they will not listen to me, says the Lord' (1 Corinthians 14:21). Tongues are a sign for unbelievers, not for believers; prophecy is for believers, not for unbelievers. If the whole church comes together and all speak in tongues, and outsiders enter, will they not say you are out of your minds? But if all prophesy and an unbeliever enters, he is convicted, he is called to account, the secrets of his heart are disclosed, and he falls on his face, worshiping God and declaring that God is really among you (1 Corinthians 14:22–25).
13.historical_context: The Isaiah 28:11–12 citation (v.21, 'by people of strange tongues') interprets the Assyrian invasion's incomprehensible foreign speech as God's judgment on Israel's failure to listen—tongues were a sign of judgment, not blessing. Paul applies this typologically: uninterpreted tongues in worship can function as a sign of judgment (driving unbelievers to think the community is mad) rather than blessing. The 'five words with my mind rather than ten thousand in a tongue' (v.19) is Paul's strongest practical statement of the intelligibility principle. The outsider's potential conversion through prophecy (vv.24–25) is the positive vision: intelligible Spirit-inspired speech has evangelistic power that ecstatic tongues lacks.
13.literary_structure: The passage applies the intelligibility criterion to the individual (pray with spirit and mind, sing with spirit and mind, vv.14–15), to corporate prayer (the Amen requires understanding, vv.16–17), and to the practical comparison (five intelligible words > ten thousand in tongue, v.19). The Isaiah citation (vv.21–22) provides OT grounding. The two scenarios (all tongues vs. all prophecy, vv.23–25) provide the practical test: what would an outsider make of this worship? The conviction and conversion of the unbeliever through prophecy is the worship's most powerful evangelistic witness.
13.theological_application: The 'five words with my mind to instruct' principle establishes that in corporate worship, the community's comprehension takes priority over individual spiritual intensity. This is the love principle applied to worship form: love does not insist on its own spiritual experience at the expense of others' understanding. The visitor or outsider's potential experience (v.23–25) is a genuine pastoral concern—the community's worship is not private religious practice but public expression of its life in the Spirit, and it has implications for those who enter its space. Worship that is inaccessible to outsiders is not necessarily wrong (worship is for God) but worship that is designed to be inaccessible is a missed opportunity for the kind of prophetic witness that produces 'God is really among you' (v.25). The outsider's prostration and confession is the highest possible testimony to the reality of the Spirit in the community's midst.
13.christology: The conviction of the outsider through prophecy—'the secrets of his heart are disclosed' (v.25)—is the Spirit's activity revealing what only God can know: the interior reality of human existence. This is a christological event in that the Spirit who searches all things (1 Corinthians 2:10) does in prophetic speech what Christ himself did in his earthly ministry when he spoke to the woman at the well about her husbands (John 4:17–19) or forgave sins to the paralytic, revealing the heart's deepest need. The outsider's 'God is really among you' is the confession that the Spirit of the risen Lord is genuinely present in this community—the same confession the prophets anticipated when they spoke of the divine presence dwelling among the nations in the last days.

26.synthesis: What then? When they come together, each one has a hymn, a lesson, a revelation, a tongue, or an interpretation—let all things be done for building up (1 Corinthians 14:26). If any speak in a tongue, let there be only two or at most three, and each in turn, and let someone interpret. If there is no one to interpret, let him keep silent in church and speak to himself and to God (1 Corinthians 14:27–28). Let two or three prophets speak, and let the others weigh what is said. If a revelation is made to another sitting there, let the first be silent. For you can all prophesy one by one so that all may learn and all may be encouraged (1 Corinthians 14:29–31). The spirits of prophets are subject to prophets, for God is not a God of confusion but of peace, as in all the churches of the saints (1 Corinthians 14:32–33).
26.historical_context: The participatory character of Corinthian worship (each one has a contribution, v.26) reflects the early church's open, Spirit-led gathering rather than the more structured leader-centered format that would develop later. Paul does not eliminate this participatory character but orders it: limits on numbers (two or three), requirements for sequential rather than simultaneous speech, the weighing of prophecy by the community. The principle that 'the spirits of prophets are subject to prophets' (v.32) is important: genuine prophetic inspiration does not produce uncontrollable behavior. The prophet who claims divine compulsion for interrupting others has not understood the Spirit's character. God's character determines the character of his Spirit's work: not chaos but peace.
26.literary_structure: The practical regulations for tongues (vv.27–28) and prophecy (vv.29–32) are parallel in structure: numerical limit (two or three) → sequential order → accountability provision (interpretation for tongues; communal weighing for prophecy). The grounding statement—'God is not a God of confusion but of peace' (v.33)—provides the theological rationale: worship that reflects the character of its God will reflect his peaceable order. The appeal to 'all the churches of the saints' establishes that this is not idiosyncratic Pauline preference but the common practice of the apostolic communities.
26.theological_application: The orderly worship instruction is an application of the love principle to worship practice: love does not insist on its own spiritual expression at the expense of communal order and others' comprehension. The regulations are minimal but clear: sequential rather than simultaneous; limited number; accountability through interpretation and evaluation. The community's freedom in worship is real (everyone may contribute) but not unlimited (everything must serve upbuilding). The 'God of peace' grounding is crucial: the character of the God being worshiped shapes the character of the worship that honors him. Communities that allow chaos in the name of spiritual freedom have misunderstood both the Spirit and the God whose character the Spirit expresses. Order in worship is not a capitulation to human control but a reflection of the divine nature.
26.christology: 'God is not a God of confusion but of peace' (v.33) reflects the character of the triune God who is himself the source of the peace Christ announced ('Peace I leave with you,' John 14:27) and the Spirit communicates ('the fruit of the Spirit is... peace,' Galatians 5:22). The Spirit does not produce in worship communities what Christ did not produce in his disciples: not chaos and competition but the peace that passes understanding. The prophets' capacity to control their own spirits (v.32) reflects the Spirit's work of self-discipline—the same Spirit who produces the fruit of self-control (Galatians 5:23) does not override the prophet's self-control in the exercise of the prophetic gift. The Spirit and the Word work together to order the community's worship around the character of the God who gives both.

34.synthesis: Women should keep silent in the churches—they are not permitted to speak but should be in submission, as the Law also says. If there is anything they desire to learn, let them ask their husbands at home, for it is shameful for a woman to speak in church (1 Corinthians 14:34–35). Or was it from them that the word of God came? Or are they the only ones it has reached? If anyone thinks he is a prophet or spiritual, he should acknowledge that what Paul is writing is a command of the Lord. If anyone does not recognize this, he is not recognized (1 Corinthians 14:36–38). So, brothers, earnestly desire to prophesy, and do not forbid speaking in tongues. But all things should be done decently and in order (1 Corinthians 14:39–40).
34.historical_context: This passage is one of the most debated in the Pauline corpus. Several observations: (1) Paul has already assumed women pray and prophesy in 11:5, so an absolute prohibition cannot be the meaning; (2) some manuscripts place vv.34–35 after verse 40, suggesting possible interpolation in some textual traditions; (3) the most contextually coherent reading is that 'speaking' refers to the evaluation/weighing of prophecy (v.29), which may have been a quasi-judicial function in the assembly. The 'ask your husbands at home' instruction makes most sense if the speaking in question is cross-examination or interrogation of prophets during the weighing process rather than prayer, prophecy, or teaching generally. The appeal to 'the Law' is unclear—the most natural reading is OT creation-order texts (Genesis 2–3) rather than a specific commandment.
34.literary_structure: The passage is inserted into the orderly worship instructions and shares their concern for order and decorum in the assembly. The rhetorical questions of verses 36–37 are directed at those who might resist Paul's instruction, and the command-of-the-Lord claim (v.37) asserts apostolic authority. The conclusion (vv.39–40) returns to the chapter's main themes: desire prophecy, do not forbid tongues, but let everything be decent and orderly. This summary provides closure to the entire chapters 12–14 discussion of spiritual gifts.
34.theological_application: The contextual complexity of this passage requires interpretive humility. The most consistent reading across 1 Corinthians (where women pray and prophesy, 11:5; where the Spirit distributes gifts without gender qualification, 12:11; where love is the supreme gift available to all, ch.13) is that Paul is addressing a specific disruptive behavior in Corinthian assembly practice rather than pronouncing a universal principle of female silence. The 'decent and orderly' (v.40) closure applies to the entire discussion: whatever the specific application, the principle is that the community's gathered worship should reflect the character of its God—peaceful, ordered, intelligible, and mutually upbuilding. The church in every generation must read these texts with attention to both their situational specificity and their enduring principles, neither flattening the particularity nor missing the universal.
34.christology: The conclusion—'earnestly desire to prophesy, do not forbid tongues, let all be decent and orderly' (vv.39–40)—is the practical summary of chapters 12–14's teaching on Spirit gifts under the lordship of Christ. The risen Lord who pours out the Spirit distributes gifts to both men and women for the community's upbuilding. The ordering of worship is ultimately in service of the Lord being honored and the community being built up—both ends that the Christ who gave himself for the church desires and the Spirit he has given pursues. The final word ('in order') is the Spirit's own character: not the disorder of the flesh but the peaceable order that reflects the character of the God who is Father, Son, and Holy Spirit in eternal, perfect communion.
